research_series.series_name: Autonomic State, HRV, Resting Heart Rate, Fatigue, Stress, Mood, and Cognition

patterns.pattern_name: Reduced vagally mediated HRV is associated with higher fatigue, stress, anxiety/depression symptoms, and lower recovery state

mediator.name: parasympathetic_vagal_activity
mediator.pathway: reduced cardiac vagal modulation and autonomic flexibility
mediator.mechanism: lower vagal tone may reflect reduced regulatory capacity, impaired recovery, and higher allostatic load

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Reduced RMSSD/HF-HRV increases the likelihood of fatigue, stress/anxiety symptoms, mood disturbance, and poor recovery state, but should be treated as a probabilistic marker rather than a direct diagnosis.

hypotheses.reason: Multiple reviews/meta-analyses and clinical studies link reduced vagally mediated HRV with fatigue, stress, anxiety/depression/PTSD symptoms, and lower recovery/wellness.

evidence.summary: Reduced vagally mediated HRV, especially RMSSD and HF-HRV, is consistently associated with fatigue, stress, anxiety/depression/PTSD symptoms, mood disturbance, and poorer recovery across healthy and clinical populations.

key_sources.title: Heart Rate Variability and Cognitive Function: A Systematic Review
key_sources.relevant_finding: HRV is associated with cognitive functioning and regulatory capacity.
key_sources.limitations: heterogeneity of HRV protocols and cognitive tasks

key_sources.title: Decreased Heart Rate Variability Is Associated with Increased Fatigue Across Different Medical Populations
key_sources.relevant_finding: Reduced HRV is associated with increased fatigue across medical populations.

key_sources.title: Stress and Heart Rate Variability: A Meta-Analysis and Review of the Literature
key_sources.relevant_finding: Stress is associated with reduced HRV and autonomic imbalance.
key_sources.limitations: heterogeneity of stressors and HRV metrics

limitations: HRV is sensitive to non-psychological confounders
Resting HRV and phasic HRV should not be merged

patterns.pattern_name: Higher resting vagally mediated HRV is associated with better executive function, working memory, and attention

mediator.name: vagal_flexibility
mediator.mechanism: higher vagally mediated HRV may index better top-down regulatory capacity and physiological flexibility

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Higher resting RMSSD/HF-HRV is associated with better executive function, working memory, and attention, but the relationship is context-dependent and not necessarily causal.

hypotheses.reason: Systematic reviews and cohort/lab studies consistently link higher vagally mediated HRV with better cognition.

evidence.summary: Higher resting vagally mediated HRV is consistently associated with better executive function, attention, working memory, and cognitive performance; lower HRV may predict cognitive decline in older adults.

key_sources.title: Heart Rate Variability and Cognitive Function: A Systematic Review
key_sources.relevant_finding: HRV is linked to executive function and cognitive performance.

key_sources.title: Heart Rate Variability and Cognition: A Narrative Systematic Review of Longitudinal Studies
key_sources.relevant_finding: Longitudinal literature suggests HRV-cognition links, especially in aging.

limitations: Some sympathetic activity may support performance under specific conditions
Effect depends on HRV metric and context

patterns.pattern_name: Acute mental stress induces sympathetic activation and vagal withdrawal

mediator.name: sympathetic_activation_and_vagal_withdrawal
mediator.mechanism: stress exposure rapidly decreases HF-HRV/RMSSD and may increase LF/HF ratio or other sympathetic-dominant indices

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: An acute stressor commonly produces rapid vagal withdrawal and sympathetic activation; recovery of parasympathetic activity after the stressor is informative about resilience/recovery.

hypotheses.reason: Meta-analyses/lab studies show robust HRV changes during acute mental stress.

evidence.summary: Acute stress reliably reduces HF-HRV/RMSSD and can increase sympathetic-dominant indices. Recovery trajectory is important and should be modeled separately from stress reactivity.

key_sources.title: Acute mental stress assessment via short term HRV analysis in healthy adults
key_sources.relevant_finding: Acute mental stress changes short-term HRV indices.

key_sources.title: Stress and Heart Rate Variability: A Meta-Analysis and Review of the Literature
key_sources.relevant_finding: Stress is associated with autonomic imbalance and reduced HRV.

limitations: Acute low HRV can be adaptive, not always pathological

patterns.pattern_name: Slow breathing and HRV biofeedback can improve vagal tone, baroreflex sensitivity, mood, and stress resilience

mediator.name: increased_vagal_tone_and_baroreflex_sensitivity
mediator.pathway: respiratory sinus arrhythmia and baroreflex modulation
mediator.mechanism: slow breathing near resonance frequency can increase HRV and baroreflex engagement

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Slow breathing and HRV biofeedback may improve autonomic regulation and mood/stress resilience, but long-term standardized evidence remains less mature.

hypotheses.reason: Intervention studies show improvements, but protocols and long-term follow-up vary.

evidence.summary: Slow breathing/HRV biofeedback interventions can increase vagal tone/baroreflex sensitivity and improve mood or stress resilience, but standardization and long-term evidence remain gaps.

key_sources.title: Preliminary Results of an Open Label Study of Heart Rate Variability Biofeedback for the Treatment of Major Depression
key_sources.relevant_finding: HRV biofeedback showed potential antidepressant effects.

key_sources.title: Effects of transcutaneous vagus nerve stimulation in individuals aged 55 years or above
key_sources.relevant_finding: Daily stimulation showed potential autonomic and well-being benefits.

limitations: Some trials are small or preliminary

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Negative affect is not always associated with lower resting HRV outside clinical samples.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Large non-clinical samples can show null findings for negative affect/resting HRV links.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: LF/HF ratio should not be treated as a simple direct measure of sympathetic-vagal balance.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: HRV metric interpretation is context-dependent and methodologically heterogeneous.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Moderate sympathetic activation may support cognition or exercise capacity in some older or disease populations.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Some evidence suggests nonlinear/inverted-U effects rather than a simple harmful effect of sympathetic activation.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: Reduced root mean square of successive differences, a time-domain HRV index reflecting vagally mediated variability.
Reduced high-frequency HRV, commonly interpreted as lower parasympathetic/vagal cardiac modulation under appropriate measurement conditions.
Resting heart rate above individual baseline, potentially reflecting stress, illness, poor recovery, sleep loss, or other physiological load.
Acute or chronic reduction in vagally mediated autonomic activity.
Increased sympathetic arousal commonly observed during acute stress, exertion, illness, or threat response.
Delayed restoration of autonomic balance after a stressor.
Subjective or inferred state of poor recovery/wellness and reduced readiness.
Decline in mental performance or perceived mental effort during sustained cognitive demand.

source_verification_queue.title: Heart rate variability as a dual-use digital biomarker: integrating clinical, AI, and operational perspectives on human performance and resilience
source_verification_queue.reason: Future-dated 2026 source should be verified before inclusion in static graph.

source_verification_queue.title: From brain to heart: cognitive performance shapes exercise-induced cardiac autonomic trajectories in older adults
source_verification_queue.reason: Future-dated 2026 source should be verified before inclusion in static graph.

source_verification_queue.title: Breathe better, live better: the science of slow breathing and heart rate variability
source_verification_queue.reason: 2025 review should be verified and assessed for source type before evidence weighting.

source_verification_queue.title: Decreased Heart Rate Variability Is Associated with Increased Fatigue Across Different Medical Populations
source_verification_queue.reason: 2025 review should be verified and source details extracted.

research_gaps: Need longitudinal and interventional studies linking autonomic changes to daily functioning and fatigue over time.
Need standardization of HRV recording protocols, respiration control, and metric interpretation.
Baroreflex sensitivity is under-studied in longitudinal and intervention designs.
Long-term cognitive effects of HRV biofeedback or vagal tone interventions remain unclear.
Directionality between chronic autonomic imbalance and subjective fatigue remains uncertain.

most_supported_patterns: Reduced RMSSD/HF-HRV is associated with increased fatigue, stress, anxiety/depression symptoms, and poorer recovery.
Higher resting vagally mediated HRV is associated with better executive function and cognitive performance.
Acute mental stress induces sympathetic activation and vagal withdrawal.
highest_value_patterns_for_inference: Reduced vagally mediated HRV as a probabilistic evidence node for allostatic load, fatigue, stress, and recovery.
Dynamic HRV reactivity/recovery as a marker of resilience and stress recovery.
HRV metric-specific edges instead of a generic HRV node.
patterns_requiring_caution: LF/HF ratio as a direct sympathetic-vagal balance metric.
Negative affect -> lower resting HRV in non-clinical samples.
Sympathetic activation as always harmful; evidence suggests nonlinear context-dependent effects.
recommended_next_queries: How do cortisol and HPA-axis dynamics interact with HRV, fatigue, anxiety, and cognition in humans?
How do respiration rate, CO2, panic symptoms, and HRV interact in anxiety and cognitive impairment?
summary.overall_assessment: The autonomic/HRV series is strong and graph-relevant. HRV should be modeled as a noisy probabilistic physiological evidence node, not a direct psychological diagnosis. Metric-specific, context-aware edges are essential.
